title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given s, return the length of the longest substring with all distinct characters.

examples.explanation: Start: max_len=0, s='abcabcbb' (n=8). i=0: j=0: substring='a', set={'a'}, length=1, max_len=1. j=1: substring='ab', set={'a','b'}, length=2, max_len=2. j=2: substring='abc', set={'a','b','c'}, length=3, max_len=3. j=3: substring='abca', set={'a','b','c'}, length=4 but set size=3, skip. j=4: substring='abcab', set size=3, skip. j=5: substring='abcabc', set size=3, skip. j=6: substring='abcabcb', set size=3, skip. j=7: substring='abcabcbb', set size=3, skip. i=1: j=1: 'b', max_len=3. j=2: 'bc', max_len=3. j=3: 'bca', set={'b','c','a'}, length=3, max_len=3. j=4: 'bcab', set size=3, skip. Continue all i up to 7. Final max_len=3.

Start: max_len=0, s='bbbbb' (n=5). i=0: j=0: substring='b', set={'b'}, length=1, max_len=1. j=1: substring='bb', set={'b'}, length=2 but set size=1, skip. j=2: 'bbb', set size=1, skip. j=3: 'bbbb', set size=1, skip. j=4: 'bbbbb', set size=1, skip. i=1: j=1: 'b', max_len=1. j=2: 'bb', skip. Continue all i. All substrings have repeating 'b'. Final max_len=1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Check all possible substrings by iterating through start and end indices, and for each substring, verify if all characters are unique using a set or array. This is straightforward but inefficient for large strings.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(min(n, m)) where m is character set size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force O(n³) Solution
ladders.desc: Complete implementation checking all possible substrings for uniqueness
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by exhaustively examining every possible substring of the input string. The intuition is straightforward: if we check every substring, we're guaranteed to find the longest one without repeating characters. We generate all substrings using nested loops for start and end indices, then for each substring, we verify if all characters are unique using a set. The algorithm works by: 1) Initialize max_length to 0. 2) Outer loop iterates start index from 0 to n-1. 3) Inner loop iterates end index from start to n-1. 4) For each substring s[start:end+1], create a set of characters. 5) If length of set equals length of substring, all characters are unique. 6) Update max_length if current substring length is greater. Time complexity is O(n³) because we have O(n²) substrings and checking each substring takes O(k) where k is substring length, worst-case O(n). Space complexity is O(min(n, m)) for the set. This connects to L1 by using the uniqueness check as a subroutine. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in substring indices, not resetting the set for each substring, and inefficient duplicate checking. Real code pattern: def longest_substring(s): max_len = 0; for i in range(len(s)): for j in range(i, len(s)): if len(set(s[i:j+1])) == j-i+1: max_len = max(max_len, j-i+1); return max_len

examples.trace: Start: max_len=0, s='abcabcbb' (n=8). i=0: j=0: substring='a', set={'a'}, length=1, max_len=1. j=1: substring='ab', set={'a','b'}, length=2, max_len=2. j=2: substring='abc', set={'a','b','c'}, length=3, max_len=3. j=3: substring='abca', set={'a','b','c'}, length=4 but set size=3, skip. j=4: substring='abcab', set size=3, skip. j=5: substring='abcabc', set size=3, skip. j=6: substring='abcabcb', set size=3, skip. j=7: substring='abcabcbb', set size=3, skip. i=1: j=1: 'b', max_len=3. j=2: 'bc', max_len=3. j=3: 'bca', set={'b','c','a'}, length=3, max_len=3. j=4: 'bcab', set size=3, skip. Continue all i up to 7. Final max_len=3.

Start: max_len=0, s='bbbbb' (n=5). i=0: j=0: substring='b', set={'b'}, length=1, max_len=1. j=1: substring='bb', set={'b'}, length=2 but set size=1, skip. j=2: 'bbb', set size=1, skip. j=3: 'bbbb', set size=1, skip. j=4: 'bbbbb', set size=1, skip. i=1: j=1: 'b', max_len=1. j=2: 'bb', skip. Continue all i. All substrings have repeating 'b'. Final max_len=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check Substring Uniqueness
ladders.desc: Determine if all characters in a substring are distinct
ladders.explanation: This subroutine checks whether a given substring contains only unique characters. The intuition is that if we can quickly verify uniqueness, we can integrate this check into the larger brute force solution. The algorithm works by: 1) Accept a substring as input. 2) Create an empty set to track seen characters. 3) Iterate through each character in the substring. 4) For each character, if it's already in the set, return False (duplicate found). 5) Otherwise, add it to the set. 6) If loop completes without duplicates, return True. Time complexity is O(k) where k is substring length, as we process each character once. Space complexity is O(min(k, m)) for the set, where m is character set size. This connects to L0 by being the core verification step in the brute force approach. Common mistakes include: not handling empty substrings, using a list instead of set for O(1) lookups, and incorrect early termination. Real code pattern: def is_unique(substring): seen = set(); for char in substring: if char in seen: return False; seen.add(char); return True

examples.trace: Input substring='abc'. Initialize seen={}. char='a': 'a' not in seen, add to seen={'a'}. char='b': 'b' not in seen, add to seen={'a','b'}. char='c': 'c' not in seen, add to seen={'a','b','c'}. Loop completes, return True.
Input substring='aba'. Initialize seen={}. char='a': 'a' not in seen, add to seen={'a'}. char='b': 'b' not in seen, add to seen={'a','b'}. char='a': 'a' is in seen, return False immediately.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate All Substrings
ladders.desc: Iterate through all possible start and end indices to create substrings
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves generating every possible substring of a string by systematically varying start and end indices. The intuition is that to examine all substrings, we need to consider every combination of starting point and length. The algorithm works by: 1) Get length n of string. 2) Outer loop: start index i from 0 to n-1. 3) Inner loop: end index j from i to n-1. 4) For each pair (i, j), extract substring s[i:j+1]. This produces all contiguous substrings. Time complexity is O(n²) since there are n(n+1)/2 substrings. Space complexity is O(1) excluding the substrings themselves. This connects to L1 by providing the substrings to check for uniqueness. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (using j < n instead of j <= n), missing single-character substrings, and generating non-contiguous sequences. Real code pattern: def generate_substrings(s): n = len(s); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): substring = s[i:j+1]; # process substring

examples.trace: Input s='abc', n=3. i=0: j=0: substring='a'. j=1: substring='ab'. j=2: substring='abc'. i=1: j=1: substring='b'. j=2: substring='bc'. i=2: j=2: substring='c'. Total 6 substrings.
Input s='ab', n=2. i=0: j=0: substring='a'. j=1: substring='ab'. i=1: j=1: substring='b'. Total 3 substrings.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested for-loops to systematically explore index pairs
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using two nested loops to iterate through all possible pairs of indices (i, j) where i <= j. The intuition is that many problems require examining all contiguous segments of a sequence, and nested loops provide a straightforward way to do this. The algorithm works by: 1) Determine the length n of the sequence. 2) Outer loop: variable i from 0 to n-1 (start index). 3) Inner loop: variable j from i to n-1 (end index). 4) For each pair, perform an operation (e.g., access elements from i to j). Time complexity is O(n²) as the inner loop runs n-i times for each i. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 by being the mechanism to generate substrings. Common mistakes include: starting inner loop at 0 instead of i (creating non-contiguous segments), incorrect range boundaries, and confusion between indices and lengths. Real code pattern: n = len(seq); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): # process seq[i:j+1]

examples.trace: Input n=3. i=0: j from 0 to 2: (0,0), (0,1), (0,2). i=1: j from 1 to 2: (1,1), (1,2). i=2: j from 2 to 2: (2,2). Total 6 pairs.
Input n=2. i=0: j from 0 to 1: (0,0), (0,1). i=1: j from 1 to 1: (1,1). Total 3 pairs.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Slicing
ladders.desc: Extract a contiguous portion of a string using start and end indices
ladders.explanation: String slicing is the operation of extracting a substring from a larger string by specifying start and end indices. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we often need to work with parts of them rather than the whole. The algorithm works by: 1) Given a string s and indices i (start, inclusive) and j (end, exclusive in some languages, inclusive adjusted). 2) In Python-style: substring = s[i:j+1] to include character at j. 3) This creates a new string containing characters from index i to j. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the substring, as it copies characters. Space complexity is O(k) for the new string. This connects to L3 by being used within nested loops to get each substring. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (using s[i:j] instead of s[i:j+1]), negative indices, and slices beyond string bounds. Real code pattern: s = 'example'; substring = s[1:4] # gets 'xam' (indices 1,2,3)

examples.trace: String s='hello', indices: 0='h',1='e',2='l',3='l',4='o'. Slice s[1:4] (since j=3, use j+1=4). Extract characters at indices 1,2,3: 'e','l','l'. Result substring='ell'.
String s='world', indices: 0='w',1='o',2='r',3='l',4='d'. Slice s[0:5] (j+1=5). Extract indices 0 to 4: 'w','o','r','l','d'. Result substring='world' (full string).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterate Through String Indices
ladders.desc: Basic loop to access each character in a string by its position
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves using a loop to traverse a string character by character using indices. The intuition is that strings are indexed sequences, and we need to access individual elements to process them. The algorithm works by: 1) Determine the length of the string using len(s). 2) Use a for-loop with index variable i ranging from 0 to len(s)-1. 3) In each iteration, access the character at s[i]. 4) Perform an operation on that character (e.g., print, compare). Time complexity is O(n) for an n-length string. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 as the basis for more complex operations like slicing. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing, going out of bounds, and confusing characters with indices. Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; for i in range(len(s)): char = s[i]; # process char

examples.trace: String s='cat', len=3. i=0: access s[0]='c'. i=1: access s[1]='a'. i=2: access s[2]='t'. Loop completes after 3 iterations.
String s='ab', len=2. i=0: access s[0]='a'. i=1: access s[1]='b'. Loop completes after 2 iterations.

approaches.name: Sliding Window with HashSet O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to define a sliding window and a hash set to track characters in the current window. Expand the window by moving the right pointer, and if a duplicate is found, shrink the window by moving the left pointer until the duplicate is removed.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(min(n, m)) where m is character set size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sliding Window with HashSet Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two pointers and a hash set to find the longest substring without repeating characters.
ladders.explanation:
This is the complete implementation of the sliding window approach with a hash set. The intuition is to maintain a window of unique characters using two pointers (left and right) that slide through the string. We use a hash set to efficiently check for duplicates in O(1) time. As we expand the window by moving the right pointer, we add characters to the set. When we encounter a duplicate (character already in set), we shrink the window from the left by removing characters from the set until the duplicate is eliminated. Throughout this process, we track the maximum window length encountered.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize left = 0, max_len = 0, and an empty hash set char_set
2. For right pointer from 0 to n-1:
   a. While s[right] is in char_set:
      - Remove s[left] from char_set
      - Increment left pointer
   b. Add s[right] to char_set
   c. Update max_len = max(max_len, right - left + 1)
3. Return max_len

Time complexity: O(n) - each character is added and removed from the set at most once
Space complexity: O(min(n, m)) where m is character set size (ASCII has 128, Unicode has much more)

Connection to L1: This builds upon the key subroutine of maintaining the sliding window with the hash set.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to update max_len after shrinking the window
2. Using while instead of if for duplicate check (while is correct to handle multiple duplicates)
3. Not handling empty string case

Real code pattern:
def longest_unique_substring(s):
    left = 0
    max_len = 0
    char_set = set()
    
    for right in range(len(s)):
        while s[right] in char_set:
            char_set.remove(s[left])
            left += 1
        char_set.add(s[right])
        max_len = max(max_len, right - left + 1)
    
    return max_len

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize left=0, max_len=0, char_set={}
Step 2: right=0, char='a', char_set={}, add 'a', char_set={'a'}, max_len=max(0,0-0+1)=1
Step 3: right=1, char='b', char_set={'a'}, 'b' not in set, add 'b', char_set={'a','b'}, max_len=max(1,1-0+1)=2
Step 4: right=2, char='c', char_set={'a','b'}, 'c' not in set, add 'c', char_set={'a','b','c'}, max_len=max(2,2-0+1)=3
Step 5: right=3, char='a', char_set={'a','b','c'}, 'a' in set, remove s[left]=s[0]='a', left=1, char_set={'b','c'}, 'a' still in set? No, add 'a', char_set={'b','c','a'}, max_len=max(3,3-1+1)=3
Step 6: right=4, char='b', char_set={'b','c','a'}, 'b' in set, remove s[1]='b', left=2, char_set={'c','a'}, 'b' not in set, add 'b', char_set={'c','a','b'}, max_len=3
Step 7: right=5, char='c', char_set={'c','a','b'}, 'c' in set, remove s[2]='c', left=3, char_set={'a','b'}, 'c' not in set, add 'c', char_set={'a','b','c'}, max_len=3
Step 8: right=6, char='b', char_set={'a','b','c'}, 'b' in set, remove s[3]='a', left=4, char_set={'b','c'}, 'b' still in set, remove s[4]='b', left=5, char_set={'c'}, 'b' not in set, add 'b', char_set={'c','b'}, max_len=3
Step 9: right=7, char='b', char_set={'c','b'}, 'b' in set, remove s[5]='c', left=6, char_set={'b'}, 'b' still in set, remove s[6]='b', left=7, char_set={}, add 'b', char_set={'b'}, max_len=3
Final result: 3

Step 1: Initialize left=0, max_len=0, char_set={}
Step 2: right=0, char='p', add 'p', char_set={'p'}, max_len=1
Step 3: right=1, char='w', add 'w', char_set={'p','w'}, max_len=2
Step 4: right=2, char='w', 'w' in set, remove s[0]='p', left=1, char_set={'w'}, 'w' still in set, remove s[1]='w', left=2, char_set={}, add 'w', char_set={'w'}, max_len=2
Step 5: right=3, char='k', add 'k', char_set={'w','k'}, max_len=2
Step 6: right=4, char='e', add 'e', char_set={'w','k','e'}, max_len=3
Step 7: right=5, char='w', 'w' in set, remove s[2]='w', left=3, char_set={'k','e'}, 'w' not in set, add 'w', char_set={'k','e','w'}, max_len=3
Final result: 3

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Sliding Window with Hash Set
ladders.desc: Implement the core sliding window logic that expands and shrinks based on character uniqueness.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine focuses on the sliding window maintenance using a hash set. The intuition is that we need to dynamically adjust our window boundaries to ensure all characters within the window are unique. When we encounter a duplicate character, we need to shrink the window from the left until that duplicate is removed. The hash set gives us O(1) lookup to check if a character is already in our current window.

Step-by-step algorithm for the subroutine:
1. Initialize left pointer at 0
2. Initialize empty hash set
3. For each new character at position right:
   a. While character is already in set:
      - Remove the character at left pointer from set
      - Move left pointer right by 1
   b. Add current character to set
   c. Current window size = right - left + 1

Time complexity: O(n) for processing all characters
Space complexity: O(k) where k is the size of character set in current window

Connection to L0: This is the engine that drives the complete solution - without this subroutine, we couldn't efficiently maintain the unique character window.

Common mistakes:
1. Using if instead of while when checking duplicates (need while to handle cases where multiple characters need removal)
2. Forgetting to remove characters when shrinking window
3. Not updating the left pointer correctly

Real code pattern:
def maintain_window(s, left, char_set, right_char):
    # Shrink window while duplicate exists
    while right_char in char_set:
        char_set.remove(s[left])
        left += 1
    
    # Add new character
    char_set.add(right_char)
    
    return left, char_set

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check if 'b' is in char_set={'a','b'} → Yes
Step 2: Remove s[left]=s[0]='a' from set, char_set={'b'}, left=1
Step 3: Check if 'b' is in char_set={'b'} → Yes
Step 4: Remove s[left]=s[1]='b' from set, char_set={}, left=2
Step 5: Check if 'b' is in char_set={} → No
Step 6: Add 'b' to set, char_set={'b'}
Result: left=2, char_set={'b'}

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two Pointer Sliding Window Technique
ladders.desc: Implement the sliding window pattern using two pointers to define a subarray.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the sliding window technique using two pointers. The intuition is that instead of checking every possible substring (which would be O(n²)), we can maintain a window that slides through the array. The left pointer marks the start of the window, and the right pointer marks the end. We expand the window by moving the right pointer, and shrink it by moving the left pointer when certain conditions are met. This technique is optimal for problems where we need to find a subarray/substring that satisfies specific constraints.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize left = 0
2. For right from 0 to n-1:
   a. Process element at right position
   b. While window violates constraint:
      - Remove element at left from consideration
      - Increment left
   c. Update answer based on current window

Time complexity: O(n) since each element is processed at most twice (added once, removed once)
Space complexity: O(1) for pointers, plus any additional data structures needed

Connection to L1: This provides the framework that the hash set implementation operates within.

Common mistakes:
1. Moving left pointer too far or not far enough
2. Not handling the while condition correctly
3. Forgetting to update the answer after window adjustment

Real code pattern:
def sliding_window(s):
    left = 0
    answer = 0
    
    for right in range(len(s)):
        # Add s[right] to window
        
        # While window violates condition:
        while condition_violated:
            # Remove s[left] from window
            left += 1
        
        # Update answer
        answer = max(answer, right - left + 1)
    
    return answer

examples.trace:
Step 1: left=0, right=0, window="a", size=1
Step 2: left=0, right=1, window="ab", size=2
Step 3: left=0, right=2, window="abc", size=3
All windows have unique characters, so no shrinking needed.

Step 1: left=0, right=0, window="a", size=1
Step 2: left=0, right=1, window="ab", size=2
Step 3: left=0, right=2, window="aba" has duplicate 'a'
   Need to shrink: remove s[left]='a', left=1
   Window="ba", size=2
Final window sizes tracked: 1, 2, 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Hash Set Operations for Character Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement and use hash set operations to track unique characters efficiently.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on using hash sets to track which characters are present in our current window. A hash set provides O(1) average time complexity for add, remove, and contains operations, making it ideal for tracking uniqueness. The intuition is that we need to quickly answer: "Is this character already in our current window?" and "How do we add/remove characters as our window changes?"

Key operations:
1. add(x): Insert element x into the set
2. remove(x): Remove element x from the set
3. contains(x): Check if x is in the set
4. size(): Get number of elements in set

For our problem, we use:
- contains() to check if a character is duplicate
- add() when expanding window
- remove() when shrinking window

Time complexity: All operations are O(1) on average
Space complexity: O(k) where k is number of unique characters in set

Connection to L2: This data structure enables the efficient window maintenance in the sliding window technique.

Common mistakes:
1. Not checking if element exists before removing (can cause KeyError)
2. Assuming set maintains insertion order (Python 3.7+ does, but shouldn't rely on it)
3. Using list instead of set for contains checks (O(n) vs O(1))

Real code pattern:
# Initialize set
char_set = set()

# Check if character exists
if char in char_set:
    # Character is duplicate

# Add character to set
char_set.add(char)

# Remove character from set
char_set.remove(char)  # May raise KeyError if not present
# Safer: char_set.discard(char)  # Doesn't raise error

examples.input: Start: set={'a','b'}, operations: contains('c'), add('c'), contains('a'), remove('a')
Start: set={'x','y','z'}, operations: contains('y'), remove('y'), contains('y'), add('w')

testCases.input: Start: set(), operations: add('1'), add('2'), add('1')
Start: set={'a','b','c'}, operations: contains('b'), remove('b'), contains('b')
Start: set={'1','2','3'}, operations: remove('2'), add('4'), contains('3')
Start: set(), operations: add('x'), add('y'), remove('x'), contains('x')

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Traversal with Index Pointers
ladders.desc: Implement basic string traversal using indices and pointer manipulation.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on the fundamental skill of traversing a string using indices and manipulating pointers. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters that can be accessed by their positions (indices). We need to be able to move through the string, access characters at specific positions, and keep track of our current position using pointer variables.

Key concepts:
1. String indexing: s[i] gives character at position i (0-based)
2. String length: len(s) gives total number of characters
3. Pointer variables: Store integer indices that point to positions in the string
4. Pointer movement: Increment/decrement pointers to move through the string

Basic operations:
1. Initialize pointer at start: i = 0
2. Move pointer forward: i += 1
3. Move pointer backward: i -= 1
4. Access character at pointer: current_char = s[i]
5. Check if pointer is within bounds: i < len(s)

Time complexity: O(1) for each access/movement
Space complexity: O(1) for pointers

Connection to L3: This provides the basic mechanism for implementing the sliding window technique.

Common mistakes:
1. Accessing index out of bounds (i >= len(s))
2. Forgetting to update pointers
3. Confusing 0-based and 1-based indexing

Real code pattern:
# Basic string traversal
s = "example"

# Forward traversal
for i in range(len(s)):
    char = s[i]
    # Process char

# Using while loop
i = 0
while i < len(s):
    char = s[i]
    # Process char
    i += 1

# Two pointer example
left = 0
right = 0
while right < len(s):
    # Process window from left to right
    right += 1
    # Maybe move left too

examples.output: Characters: h(0), e(1), l(2), l(3), o(4)

examples.input: "abc" with left=0, right=0, expand right to 2
examples.trace:
Step 1: left=0, right=0, window=s[0:1]="a"
Step 2: right=1, window=s[0:2]="ab"
Step 3: right=2, window=s[0:3]="abc"

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Character Uniqueness
ladders.desc: Learn basic string concepts and what it means for characters to be unique.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational level introduces strings and the concept of character uniqueness. A string is a sequence of characters. Characters can be letters, digits, symbols, or spaces. Character uniqueness means that no character appears more than once in a given sequence.

Key concepts:
1. String: Ordered collection of characters, e.g., "apple", "123", "a b c"
2. Character: Single symbol, e.g., 'a', '1', ' '
3. Substring: A contiguous sequence of characters within a string
4. Unique characters: All characters in a sequence are different from each other

Examples:
- "abc" has all unique characters (a, b, c are all different)
- "apple" does not have all unique characters ('p' appears twice)
- "a b" has unique characters ('a', ' ', 'b' are all different)

To check if characters are unique in a substring:
1. Look at each character in the substring
2. Compare it with all other characters in the substring
3. If any character appears more than once, they are not unique

Connection to L4: This understanding is prerequisite for implementing algorithms that find substrings with unique characters.

Common beginner mistakes:
1. Thinking uppercase and lowercase are the same (they're different: 'A' ≠ 'a')
2. Forgetting that spaces are characters too
3. Confusing substring with subsequence (substring must be contiguous)

Real code pattern:
# Basic uniqueness check for a string
def all_unique(s):
    for i in range(len(s)):
        for j in range(i + 1, len(s)):
            if s[i] == s[j]:
                return False
    return True

# Example usage
print(all_unique("abc"))  # True
print(all_unique("aba"))  # False

examples.trace:
Step 1: Check 'c' vs 'a' → different
Step 2: Check 'c' vs 't' → different
Step 3: Check 'a' vs 't' → different
All comparisons are different, so all characters are unique.

Step 1: Check 'b' vs 'o' → different
Step 2: Check 'b' vs 'o' (second) → different
Step 3: Check 'b' vs 'k' → different
Step 4: Check first 'o' vs second 'o' → SAME!
Found duplicate 'o', so characters are not all unique.

testCases.expected: True (A, space, a are all different)

approaches.name: Sliding Window with HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Similar to the hash set approach, but use a hash map to store the last seen index of each character. When a duplicate is encountered, directly jump the left pointer to the index after the last occurrence, optimizing window shrinkage.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(min(n, m)) where m is character set size

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters using Sliding Window with HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution implementing the sliding window with hashmap approach to find the longest substring with all distinct characters.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the longest contiguous substring where all characters are unique. The sliding window with hashmap approach provides an optimal O(n) solution by maintaining a window of unique characters and efficiently adjusting it when duplicates are found. The intuition is to track the last seen index of each character in a hashmap, allowing us to jump the left window boundary directly to the position after the last occurrence when a duplicate is encountered, rather than moving it one step at a time. This eliminates redundant checks and ensures each character is processed at most twice. The algorithm initializes an empty hashmap to store character-to-last-index mappings, and two pointers (left and right) representing the current window. As we iterate through the string with the right pointer, we check if the current character exists in the hashmap and if its last index is within the current window (i.e., >= left). If so, we move left to index after the last occurrence (hashmap[char] + 1) to exclude the duplicate. Then, we update the hashmap with the current character's index and update the maximum window length. The time complexity is O(n) since each character is processed once, and space complexity is O(min(n, m)) where m is the character set size (e.g., 26 for lowercase English letters). This approach is efficient because it avoids rescanning characters and directly shrinks the window optimally. Common mistakes include not checking if the last occurrence is within the current window (which could incorrectly move left backward) and forgetting to update the hashmap even when no duplicate is found. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the key subroutine of updating the window when duplicates are found into the complete solution.

examples.trace: Initialize: left=0, max_len=0, hashmap={}. Process 'a' (index 0): not in hashmap, hashmap={'a':0}, window='a', max_len=1. Process 'b' (index 1): not in hashmap, hashmap={'a':0,'b':1}, window='ab', max_len=2. Process 'c' (index 2): not in hashmap, hashmap={'a':0,'b':1,'c':2}, window='abc', max_len=3. Process 'a' (index 3): in hashmap at index 0, which is >= left=0, so move left to 0+1=1, hashmap={'a':3,'b':1,'c':2}, window='bca', max_len remains 3. Process 'b' (index 4): in hashmap at index 1, which is >= left=1, so move left to 1+1=2, hashmap={'a':3,'b':4,'c':2}, window='cab', max_len remains 3. Process 'c' (index 5): in hashmap at index 2, which is >= left=2, so move left to 2+1=3, hashmap={'a':3,'b':4,'c':5}, window='abc', max_len remains 3. Process 'b' (index 6): in hashmap at index 4, which is >= left=3, so move left to 4+1=5, hashmap={'a':3,'b':6,'c':5}, window='cb', max_len remains 3. Process 'b' (index 7): in hashmap at index 6, which is >= left=5, so move left to 6+1=7, hashmap={'a':3,'b':7,'c':5}, window='b', max_len remains 3. Final max_len=3.

Initialize: left=0, max_len=0, hashmap={}. Process 'p' (index 0): not in hashmap, hashmap={'p':0}, window='p', max_len=1. Process 'w' (index 1): not in hashmap, hashmap={'p':0,'w':1}, window='pw', max_len=2. Process 'w' (index 2): in hashmap at index 1, which is >= left=0, so move left to 1+1=2, hashmap={'p':0,'w':2}, window='w', max_len remains 2. Process 'k' (index 3): not in hashmap, hashmap={'p':0,'w':2,'k':3}, window='wk', max_len=2. Process 'e' (index 4): not in hashmap, hashmap={'p':0,'w':2,'k':3,'e':4}, window='wke', max_len=3. Process 'w' (index 5): in hashmap at index 2, which is >= left=2, so move left to 2+1=3, hashmap={'p':0,'w':5,'k':3,'e':4}, window='kew', max_len remains 3. Final max_len=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Updating Window on Duplicate Detection with HashMap
ladders.desc: Implement the core logic to adjust the sliding window when a duplicate character is found using a hashmap of last seen indices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the critical step in the sliding window approach: when we encounter a character that already exists in the current window, we must efficiently shrink the window to maintain uniqueness. The intuition is that by storing the last seen index of each character in a hashmap, we can instantly know where the duplicate occurred and jump the left boundary of the window to just after that index, rather than incrementing left one by one. This optimization reduces the time complexity from O(n^2) to O(n). The algorithm for this subroutine involves: given a current character 'c' at index 'right', a hashmap 'last_seen' mapping characters to their most recent indices, and a left pointer 'left' representing the start of the window, we check if 'c' is in 'last_seen' and if its last index is within the window (i.e., last_seen[c] >= left). If true, we update left to last_seen[c] + 1 to exclude the old occurrence. Then, we update last_seen[c] to 'right' to record the new occurrence. This ensures the window always contains unique characters. The time complexity for this subroutine is O(1) per character, and space complexity is O(min(n, m)) for the hashmap. Common mistakes include not verifying that the last index is within the current window (which could cause left to move backward incorrectly) and forgetting to update the hashmap even when no duplicate is found. The connection to L0 is that this subroutine is repeatedly called for each character in the full problem to maintain the sliding window. Real code pattern: if char in last_seen and last_seen[char] >= left: left = last_seen[char] + 1; last_seen[char] = right.

examples.input: Window: left=0, right=3, last_seen={'a':0,'b':1,'c':2}, char='a' at index 3
examples.trace: Check char='a' at index 3: in last_seen at index 0, which is >= left=0, so move left to 0+1=1. Update last_seen['a'] to 3. Window now starts at index 1.

examples.input: Window: left=2, right=5, last_seen={'w':2,'k':3,'e':4}, char='w' at index 5
examples.trace: Check char='w' at index 5: in last_seen at index 2, which is >= left=2, so move left to 2+1=3. Update last_seen['w'] to 5. Window now starts at index 3.

testCases.input: left=0, right=1, last_seen={'a':0}, char='b' at index 1
left=0, right=1, last_seen={'a':0}, char='a' at index 1
left=3, right=4, last_seen={'x':2}, char='x' at index 4
left=5, right=6, last_seen={'z':3}, char='z' at index 6
left=0, right=2, last_seen={'p':0,'q':1}, char='r' at index 2
left=1, right=3, last_seen={'a':0,'b':1,'c':2}, char='b' at index 3
left=4, right=7, last_seen={'m':4,'n':5,'o':6}, char='m' at index 7
left=0, right=0, last_seen={}, char='a' at index 0
left=2, right=4, last_seen={'d':1}, char='d' at index 4
left=10, right=12, last_seen={'e':9,'f':10,'g':11}, char='f' at index 12

ladders.title: Core Logic — Sliding Window Technique for Unique Character Substrings
ladders.desc: Implement the sliding window algorithm to maintain a window of unique characters and expand/shrink it efficiently.
ladders.explanation: The sliding window technique is a fundamental pattern for solving substring problems efficiently. The intuition is to maintain a dynamic window (represented by left and right pointers) that always satisfies a condition—in this case, containing all unique characters. As we expand the window by moving the right pointer to include new characters, we check if the condition is violated (i.e., a duplicate is found). If so, we shrink the window by moving the left pointer until the condition is restored. This allows us to explore all possible substrings in O(n) time instead of O(n^2) by avoiding redundant checks. The algorithm initializes left=0 and iterates right from 0 to n-1. For each character at right, if it's not in the current window (checked via a data structure like a set or hashmap), we add it and update the maximum window size. If it is a duplicate, we move left forward until the duplicate is removed from the window. The key insight is that the window only moves forward, ensuring each character is processed at most twice. Time complexity is O(n) because each character is added and removed at most once, and space complexity is O(min(n, m)) for storing window characters. Common mistakes include using a data structure that doesn't allow O(1) duplicate checks (e.g., list instead of set) or incorrectly updating the window when shrinking. The connection to L1 is that this level introduces the overall sliding window flow, while L1 optimizes the shrinkage using a hashmap. Real code pattern: left=0, max_len=0, window_set=set(); for right in range(n): while s[right] in window_set: remove s[left] from window_set, left+=1; add s[right] to window_set; max_len=max(max_len, right-left+1).

examples.trace: Initialize: left=0, max_len=0, window_set={}. Right=0, char='a': not in set, add, window_set={'a'}, max_len=1. Right=1, char='b': not in set, add, window_set={'a','b'}, max_len=2. Right=2, char='c': not in set, add, window_set={'a','b','c'}, max_len=3. Right=3, char='a': in set, so while loop: remove s[left=0]='a', left=1, window_set={'b','c'}; char='a' not in set now, add, window_set={'b','c','a'}, max_len remains 3. Right=4, char='b': in set, while: remove s[left=1]='b', left=2, window_set={'c','a'}; char='b' not in set, add, window_set={'c','a','b'}, max_len remains 3. Continue similarly, final max_len=3.

Initialize: left=0, max_len=0, window_set={}. Right=0, char='p': not in set, add, window_set={'p'}, max_len=1. Right=1, char='w': not in set, add, window_set={'p','w'}, max_len=2. Right=2, char='w': in set, while: remove s[left=0]='p', left=1, window_set={'w'}; char='w' still in set, remove s[left=1]='w', left=2, window_set={}; char='w' not in set, add, window_set={'w'}, max_len remains 2. Right=3, char='k': not in set, add, window_set={'w','k'}, max_len=2. Right=4, char='e': not in set, add, window_set={'w','k','e'}, max_len=3. Right=5, char='w': in set, while: remove s[left=2]='w', left=3, window_set={'k','e'}; char='w' not in set, add, window_set={'k','e','w'}, max_len remains 3. Final max_len=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Checking for Duplicates in a Dynamic Collection
ladders.desc: Implement a function to detect if a character is a duplicate in a dynamically changing collection (e.g., set or hashmap).
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on efficiently checking for duplicates as we add and remove characters from a collection, which is essential for maintaining the sliding window's uniqueness condition. The intuition is that we need a data structure that supports O(1) average-time insert, delete, and lookup operations to quickly determine if a character is already present. A hash set is ideal for this because it stores unique elements and provides constant-time operations. The algorithm involves: maintaining a set 'seen' that contains the characters currently in the window. When we want to add a new character 'c', we first check if 'c' is in 'seen'. If it is, then 'c' is a duplicate, and we must remove characters from the left of the window until 'c' is no longer in 'seen' to restore uniqueness. If it is not in 'seen', we can add it directly. This process ensures that the set always reflects the current window's characters. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, and space complexity is O(min(n, m)) for the set. Common mistakes include using a list or array for 'seen', which would require O(n) time for lookups, leading to O(n^2) overall complexity. Another mistake is not properly removing characters when shrinking the window, which can leave stale data in the set. The connection to L2 is that this duplicate checking mechanism is embedded within the sliding window loop to decide when to shrink the window. Real code pattern: seen = set(); if char in seen: while char in seen: seen.remove(s[left]); left += 1; seen.add(char).

examples.trace: Check if 'a' is in seen={'a','b','c'}: yes, it's a duplicate. Enter while loop: remove s[left=0]='a' from seen, seen becomes {'b','c'}, left=1. Check if 'a' is in seen={'b','c'}: no, exit loop. Add 'a' to seen, seen becomes {'b','c','a'}.

Check if 'w' is in seen={'w','k','e'}: yes, duplicate. While loop: remove s[left=2]='w' from seen, seen becomes {'k','e'}, left=3. Check if 'w' is in seen={'k','e'}: no, exit loop. Add 'w' to seen, seen becomes {'k','e','w'}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Adding and Removing Elements from a Hash Set
ladders.desc: Practice fundamental operations on a hash set: insert, delete, and check membership.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the basic operations of a hash set, which is a critical data structure for efficient duplicate detection. A hash set is a collection that stores unique elements and provides average O(1) time complexity for adding, removing, and checking if an element exists. The intuition is that it uses a hash function to map elements to indices in an underlying array, allowing quick access. In Python, this is implemented as the 'set' data type. The key operations are: add(element) to insert an element if it's not already present, remove(element) to delete an element, and 'element in set' to check membership. These operations are essential because, in the sliding window algorithm, we need to quickly update the set as the window moves: add new characters when expanding the window and remove old characters when shrinking it. Time complexity for each operation is O(1) on average, and space complexity is O(n) for storing n unique elements. Common mistakes include confusing set with list (which has O(n) membership checks) or not handling cases where remove is called on an element not in the set (which raises an error; use discard() to avoid this). The connection to L3 is that these set operations are used to maintain the collection of characters in the window. Real code pattern: my_set = set(); my_set.add('a'); 'a' in my_set returns True; my_set.remove('a'); my_set.discard('b') does nothing if 'b' not present.

examples.input: Start with empty set, add 'x', check if 'x' in set, add 'y', remove 'x', check if 'x' in set
examples.output: Set after adds: {'x','y'}, after remove: {'y'}, checks: True then False
examples.trace: Initialize set = {}. Add 'x': set becomes {'x'}. Check 'x' in set: True. Add 'y': set becomes {'x','y'}. Remove 'x': set becomes {'y'}. Check 'x' in set: False.

examples.input: Set initially {'a','b','c'}, check 'd' in set, add 'd', remove 'b', check 'b' in set
examples.output: Checks: False then False, final set: {'a','c','d'}
examples.trace: Initial set = {'a','b','c'}. Check 'd' in set: False. Add 'd': set becomes {'a','b','c','d'}. Remove 'b': set becomes {'a','c','d'}. Check 'b' in set: False.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings and Character Iteration
ladders.desc: Learn the basics of strings as sequences of characters and how to iterate through them.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces strings, which are fundamental to the problem. A string is a sequence of characters, such as letters, digits, or symbols. In programming, strings are typically immutable and indexed starting from 0. The intuition is that to solve substring problems, we need to access individual characters and traverse the string from start to end. Basic operations include: getting the length of a string, accessing a character at a specific index, and iterating through all characters using a loop. For example, in Python, len(s) returns the number of characters, s[i] gives the character at index i, and 'for char in s:' iterates over each character. This is essential because the sliding window algorithm requires processing each character in the string to build substrings. Time complexity for these operations is O(1) for access and O(n) for full iteration, where n is the string length. Space complexity is O(1) for iteration if no extra storage is used. Common mistakes include confusing string indices (e.g., off-by-one errors) or treating strings as mutable (they are immutable in many languages). The connection to L4 is that once we can iterate through strings, we can apply set operations to characters as we go. Real code pattern: s = 'hello'; length = len(s); first_char = s[0]; for char in s: print(char).

examples.input: String s='abc', iterate and print each character with index
examples.output: Index 0: a, Index 1: b, Index 2: c
examples.trace: String s has length 3. Use for loop with index: i=0, char=s[0]='a', print. i=1, char='b', print. i=2, char='c', print.

examples.input: String s='test', get length and access first and last characters
examples.trace: len(s) returns 4. s[0] is 't'. s[3] is 't' (since indices are 0-based).

testCases.input: s='test string', iterate and list chars

approaches.name: Sliding Window with Array O(n)
approaches.summary: Use an array of fixed size (e.g., 128 for ASCII) to track the last seen index of characters, similar to the hash map approach. This leverages direct indexing for faster lookups, assuming a limited character set like ASCII.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) if character set size is constant

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters using Sliding Window with Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution implementing the sliding window approach with a fixed-size array for character tracking.
ladders.explanation: This solution finds the longest substring with all distinct characters using a sliding window technique optimized with a fixed-size array. The intuition is to maintain a window [left, right] that always contains unique characters. We use an array of size 128 (for ASCII characters) where each index represents a character's ASCII value, storing the last seen position of that character. As we iterate through the string with the right pointer, we check if the current character has been seen within the current window by comparing its last seen index with the left pointer. If it has been seen (lastSeen[char] >= left), we move the left pointer to one position after that last occurrence to eliminate the duplicate. We update the last seen index for the current character and compute the window length (right - left + 1) to track the maximum. This approach works because it efficiently shrinks the window only when necessary, ensuring O(n) time complexity. The array provides O(1) lookups and updates, making it faster than a hash map for limited character sets. Common mistakes include not updating the left pointer correctly when a duplicate is found (e.g., moving it to lastSeen[char] instead of lastSeen[char] + 1) or forgetting to initialize the array with sentinel values like -1. This feeds into the overall problem by providing a complete, optimized solution that leverages direct indexing for performance.

examples.trace: Initialize left = 0, maxLen = 0, lastSeen array with -1. Iterate right from 0 to 7: right=0 char='a' lastSeen[97]=-1 (>= left? no) update lastSeen[97]=0, window=1, maxLen=1. right=1 char='b' lastSeen[98]=-1 (no) update lastSeen[98]=1, window=2, maxLen=2. right=2 char='c' lastSeen[99]=-1 (no) update lastSeen[99]=2, window=3, maxLen=3. right=3 char='a' lastSeen[97]=0 (>= left? yes) left = lastSeen[97]+1 = 1, update lastSeen[97]=3, window=3, maxLen=3. right=4 char='b' lastSeen[98]=1 (>= left? yes) left = lastSeen[98]+1 = 2, update lastSeen[98]=4, window=3, maxLen=3. right=5 char='c' lastSeen[99]=2 (>= left? yes) left = lastSeen[99]+1 = 3, update lastSeen[99]=5, window=3, maxLen=3. right=6 char='b' lastSeen[98]=4 (>= left? yes) left = lastSeen[98]+1 = 5, update lastSeen[98]=6, window=2, maxLen=3. right=7 char='b' lastSeen[98]=6 (>= left? yes) left = lastSeen[98]+1 = 7, update lastSeen[98]=7, window=1, maxLen=3. Return maxLen=3.

Initialize left = 0, maxLen = 0, lastSeen array with -1. Iterate right from 0 to 5: right=0 char='p' lastSeen[112]=-1 (>= left? no) update lastSeen[112]=0, window=1, maxLen=1. right=1 char='w' lastSeen[119]=-1 (no) update lastSeen[119]=1, window=2, maxLen=2. right=2 char='w' lastSeen[119]=1 (>= left? yes) left = lastSeen[119]+1 = 2, update lastSeen[119]=2, window=1, maxLen=2. right=3 char='k' lastSeen[107]=-1 (>= left? no) update lastSeen[107]=3, window=2, maxLen=2. right=4 char='e' lastSeen[101]=-1 (no) update lastSeen[101]=4, window=3, maxLen=3. right=5 char='w' lastSeen[119]=2 (>= left? yes) left = lastSeen[119]+1 = 3, update lastSeen[119]=5, window=3, maxLen=3. Return maxLen=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Sliding Window with Array Updates
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that updates the sliding window and array based on character occurrences.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the iterative process of expanding the window with the right pointer and conditionally shrinking it with the left pointer using the array. The intuition is that for each new character, we check if it has appeared in the current window by comparing its last seen index (from the array) with the left pointer. If lastSeen[char] >= left, it means the character is a duplicate within the window, so we move left to lastSeen[char] + 1 to exclude the previous occurrence. Then, we update the array with the new index for this character and compute the current window length. This ensures the window always contains unique characters. The algorithm involves initializing left=0 and an array of size 128 with -1, then looping right from 0 to n-1. For each char, if lastSeen[char] >= left, set left = lastSeen[char] + 1. Update lastSeen[char] = right. Compute window length as right - left + 1 and update maxLen if larger. Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, space is O(1) due to fixed array. This feeds into L0 by providing the main logic that drives the solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in left pointer updates or not handling the initial -1 values correctly. Real code pattern: initialize array, loop, check condition, update left, update array, compute length.

examples.trace: Initialize left=0, lastSeen array with -1. right=0 char='a': lastSeen[97]=-1 (>=0? no) left stays 0, update lastSeen[97]=0, window=1. right=1 char='b': lastSeen[98]=-1 (no) left stays 0, update lastSeen[98]=1, window=2. right=2 char='c': lastSeen[99]=-1 (no) left stays 0, update lastSeen[99]=2, window=3. Max window=3.

Initialize left=0, lastSeen array with -1. right=0 char='a': lastSeen[97]=-1 (>=0? no) left stays 0, update lastSeen[97]=0, window=1. right=1 char='b': lastSeen[98]=-1 (no) left stays 0, update lastSeen[98]=1, window=2. right=2 char='a': lastSeen[97]=0 (>=0? yes) left = 0+1 = 1, update lastSeen[97]=2, window=2. Max window=2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Using Array to Track Last Seen Indices for Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Implement the mechanism that uses a fixed-size array to store and check last seen positions of characters.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the array-based tracking system. The intuition is to use direct indexing via character ASCII values to store the most recent index where each character appeared. This allows O(1) time to check if a character has been seen before and where. We initialize an array of size 128 (covering standard ASCII) with -1, indicating no occurrence yet. As we process characters, we update the array at the index corresponding to the character's ASCII value with the current position. To detect if a character is a duplicate in the current window, we compare its stored last seen index with the left boundary of the window. If lastSeen[char] >= left, it means the character appeared at or after the window start, so it's a duplicate. This logic is key to efficiently managing the sliding window without rescanning. The algorithm involves creating the array, and for each character, performing the check and update. Time complexity is O(1) per operation, space is O(1) due to fixed size. This feeds into L1 by providing the data structure that enables the window adjustments. Common mistakes include using wrong array size (e.g., 256 for extended ASCII) or not initializing with -1. Real code pattern: array = [-1]*128, for each char, index = ord(char), check array[index], update array[index] = current_position.

examples.output: Last seen indices after processing: a:3, b:1, c:2
examples.trace: Initialize array with -1. Process 'a' at index 0: array[97] = 0. Process 'b' at index 1: array[98] = 1. Process 'c' at index 2: array[99] = 2. Process 'a' at index 3: array[97] was 0, update to 3. Final array at indices 97,98,99: 3,1,2.

examples.output: Last seen indices after processing: a:1, b:3
examples.trace: Initialize array with -1. Process 'a' at index 0: array[97] = 0. Process 'a' at index 1: array[97] was 0, update to 1. Process 'b' at index 2: array[98] = 2. Process 'b' at index 3: array[98] was 2, update to 3. Final array at indices 97,98: 1,3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sliding Window Concept for Substring Problems
ladders.desc: Implement a basic sliding window that expands and contracts to maintain a condition.
ladders.explanation: This level introduces the sliding window technique, a fundamental pattern for substring problems. The intuition is to maintain a dynamic window [left, right] that represents a substring, adjusting its boundaries based on certain conditions (e.g., uniqueness of characters). We start with left=0 and expand the window by moving right from 0 to n-1. When a condition is violated (e.g., a duplicate character is found), we shrink the window by moving left until the condition is restored. This avoids recomputing from scratch and achieves linear time. For this sub-problem, the condition is that all characters in the window are distinct. The algorithm involves initializing left=0 and maxLen=0, then iterating right. At each step, if adding s[right] causes a duplicate, move left forward until uniqueness is regained. Update maxLen with the window size (right - left + 1). Time complexity is O(n) as each character is processed at most twice, space is O(1) if using a simple counter. This feeds into L2 by providing the framework that uses the array for efficient duplicate checks. Common mistakes include not updating left correctly or using nested loops inefficiently. Real code pattern: left=0, for right in range(n): while duplicate: left+=1, update maxLen.

examples.trace: left=0, maxLen=0. right=0 char='a': no duplicate, window=[a], size=1, maxLen=1. right=1 char='b': no duplicate, window=[a,b], size=2, maxLen=2. right=2 char='c': no duplicate, window=[a,b,c], size=3, maxLen=3.

left=0, maxLen=0. right=0 char='a': no duplicate, window=[a], size=1, maxLen=1. right=1 char='b': no duplicate, window=[a,b], size=2, maxLen=2. right=2 char='a': duplicate 'a' found, move left to 1 (skip first 'a'), window=[b,a], size=2, maxLen=2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking for Duplicate Characters in a String
ladders.desc: Implement a function to detect if a character repeats in a given substring.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental operation of duplicate detection within a substring. The intuition is to determine if a specific character appears more than once in a sequence, which is essential for maintaining uniqueness in the sliding window. We can approach this by iterating through the substring and using a data structure (like a set or array) to track seen characters. For efficiency, we assume a limited character set (e.g., ASCII) and use a boolean array of size 128, where each index represents a character. As we scan the substring, if the array entry for the current character is true, it means we've seen it before, indicating a duplicate. Otherwise, we mark it as seen. The algorithm involves initializing an array with False, then for each character in the substring, check array[ord(char)]; if true, return duplicate found; else, set it to true. Time complexity is O(k) for a substring of length k, space is O(1) due to fixed array. This feeds into L3 by providing the condition check for the sliding window. Common mistakes include not resetting the array for each check or using incorrect array indices. Real code pattern: seen = [False]*128, for char in substring: idx = ord(char), if seen[idx]: return True, else seen[idx] = True, return False.

examples.trace: Initialize seen array with False. Check 'a': idx=97, seen[97] is False, set seen[97]=True. Check 'b': idx=98, seen[98] is False, set seen[98]=True. Check 'c': idx=99, seen[99] is False, set seen[99]=True. No duplicates found, return False.

Initialize seen array with False. Check 'a': idx=97, seen[97] is False, set seen[97]=True. Check 'b': idx=98, seen[98] is False, set seen[98]=True. Check 'a': idx=97, seen[97] is True, duplicate found, return True.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Iterating Through a String and Accessing Characters
ladders.desc: Basic operations: loop through a string and access individual characters by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level covers the simplest prerequisite: how to traverse a string and work with its characters. The intuition is that a string is a sequence of characters, and we can access each character using its position (index). In programming, strings are typically zero-indexed, meaning the first character is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. To iterate through a string, we use a loop (e.g., for or while) that goes from index 0 to length-1. At each step, we can retrieve the character using array-like notation (e.g., s[i]) and perform operations like printing or checking its value. This is essential for any string manipulation algorithm. The algorithm involves getting the length of the string (n), then for i from 0 to n-1, access s[i]. Time complexity is O(n) for the traversal, space is O(1) if no extra storage is used. This feeds into L4 by providing the basic mechanism to scan substrings for duplicates. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., going up to n instead of n-1) or misunderstanding zero-indexing. Real code pattern: n = len(s), for i in range(n): char = s[i], process char.

examples.trace: String length = 3. i=0: s[0] = 'a'. i=1: s[1] = 'b'. i=2: s[2] = 'c'. End of loop.

examples.output: Characters: h, e, l, l, o
examples.trace: String length = 5. i=0: s[0] = 'h'. i=1: s[1] = 'e'. i=2: s[2] = 'l'. i=3: s[3] = 'l'. i=4: s[4] = 'o'. End of loop.
